literal: The Yellow Emperor was a son of Shaodian; his surname was Gongsun, and his personal name was Xuanyuan.
idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor was a son of the house of Shaodian; his clan name was Gongsun, and he was called Xuanyuan.

literal: Born with spiritual potency, in infancy able to speak, in youth quick and orderly, grown thick yet keen, mature wise and clear.
idiomatic: From birth he showed a numinous nature; while still a babe he could speak; as a boy he was quick to learn; grown up he was steady yet perceptive; as a man he was wise and discerning.

literal: In the time of Xuanyuan, the house of Shennong declined generation by generation.
idiomatic: In Xuanyuan’s day the Shennong lineage had waned for many generations.

literal: The feudal lords attacked one another, cruelly mistreating the common people, yet Shennong could not chastise them.
idiomatic: The regional lords raided one another and tyrannized the people, and Shennong lacked the power to punish them.

literal: Thereupon Xuanyuan drilled in the use of arms and armour to punish those who did not come to court; the lords all came as guests and submitted.
idiomatic: Then Xuanyuan trained his forces in weapons and armour to chastise those who withheld tribute, and the lords came in homage and submitted to him.

literal: Among them Chiyou was the most violent; none could attack him.
idiomatic: Chiyou was the worst of them—so violent that no one could bring him down.

literal: The Flame Emperor wished to coerce the feudal lords; the lords all turned to Xuanyuan.
idiomatic: The Flame Emperor tried to bully the regional lords, and they all rallied to Xuanyuan.

literal: Xuanyuan thereupon cultivated virtue and roused the army, regulated the five vapours, planted the five grains, comforted the myriad peoples, measured the four quarters, drilled bears, brown bears, pixiu, panthers, and tigers, and with them fought the Flame Emperor in the wilds of Banquan.
idiomatic: Xuanyuan then cultivated his virtue and mobilized his hosts, set in order the five phases, sowed the five grains, soothed the myriad peoples, surveyed the four quarters, and trained bears, brown bears, leopard-cats, lynxes, and tigers for war, meeting the Flame Emperor on the field of Banquan.

literal: After three battles he then obtained his aim.
idiomatic: After three engagements he prevailed.

literal: Chiyou made rebellion and did not obey the emperor’s command.
idiomatic: Chiyou rose in revolt and defied the sovereign’s orders.

literal: Thereupon the Yellow Emperor summoned troops from the feudal lords, fought Chiyou in the wilds of Zhuolu, and there captured and killed Chiyou.
idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor then called up the armies of the lords, met Chiyou in the wilderness of Zhuolu, captured him, and put him to death.

literal: The feudal lords all honored Xuanyuan as Son of Heaven, replacing Shennong; this is the Yellow Emperor.
idiomatic: The lords acclaimed Xuanyuan Son of Heaven in Shennong’s place—this ruler is called the Yellow Emperor.

literal: Wherever under Heaven there were those who did not submit, the Yellow Emperor followed and chastised them; where he had pacified he departed; he opened mountains and cut roads, and never dwelt at rest.
idiomatic: He campaigned against every corner of the realm that refused submission; once pacified he moved on, cutting passes through hills and carving roads, never settling long in one place.

literal: East he reached to the sea, climbed Mount Wan, and came to Mount Dai.
idiomatic: To the east he went as far as the sea, ascending Mount Wan and reaching the sacred peak of Tai.

literal: West he reached to Hollow Tong, climbed Chicken Head.
idiomatic: To the west he reached Hollow Tong and ascended Chicken Head Mountain.

literal: South he reached to the Yangzi, climbed Bear and Xiang.
idiomatic: To the south he reached the Yangzi and climbed Bear and Xiang peaks.

literal: North he drove out the Hunyu, joined tallies at Mount Fu, and built a town on the slope of Zhuolu.
idiomatic: To the north he drove off the Hunyu tribes, met the lords to join tallies at Mount Fu, and founded his seat on the southern slopes of Zhuolu.

literal: Moving and migrating, coming and going, without constant place, he took armies and soldiers as camp and guard.
idiomatic: He wandered without a fixed capital, making his camps and palisades wherever his army halted.

literal: Official titles were all named from clouds; he made cloud masters.
idiomatic: He named every office after a cloud and organized his officers as cloud marshals.

literal: He set left and right great overseers to supervise the myriad states.
idiomatic: He appointed paired grand overseers of the left and right to watch over the myriad domains.

literal: The myriad states were harmonious, and therefore offerings to ghosts and spirits, mountains and rivers, and feng and shan sacrifices became numerous.
idiomatic: The myriad regions lived in harmony, so sacrifices to gods, spirits, mountains, and rivers—and the great feng and shan rites—multiplied under his reign.

literal: He obtained the precious tripod and welcomed the sun to push the counting-rods.
idiomatic: He gained the sacred tripod and, facing the sunrise, worked the divining stalks to reckon the calendar.

literal: He raised Feng Hou, Li Mu, Chang Xian, and Da Hong to govern the people.
idiomatic: He promoted Feng Hou, Li Mu, Chang Xian, and Da Hong to administer the people.

literal: He followed the cords of Heaven and Earth, the divinations of dark and bright, the explanations of death and life, and the difficulties of survival and loss.
idiomatic: He followed the patterns of heaven and earth, read the omens of hidden and manifest powers, studied doctrines of life and death, and weighed the crises of survival and ruin.

literal: In season he sowed the hundred grains and grasses and trees, tamed birds, beasts, insects, and moths; he spread nets over sun, moon, stars, planets, waves, earth, stone, metal, and jade; he labored heart, strength, ears, and eyes; he regulated use of water, fire, materials, and things.
idiomatic: He sowed the hundred grains and nurtured plants in their seasons; he gentled birds, beasts, and crawling things; he charted sun, moon, stars, tides, soil, stone, metal, and jade; he taxed mind, sinew, ear, and eye; and he husbanded water, fire, wood, and every resource.

literal: There was an auspicious sign of the virtue of earth; therefore he was titled Yellow Emperor.
idiomatic: An omen of earth’s virtue appeared, so he took the style Yellow Emperor.

literal: The Yellow Emperor had twenty-five sons; of them fourteen received surnames.
idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor fathered twenty-five sons, fourteen of whom founded separate surnames.

literal: The Yellow Emperor dwelt on the hill of Xuanyuan and took a woman of West Mound as wife; this is Leizu, who was the Yellow Emperor’s principal consort and bore two sons; afterward both lines possessed the realm: the first was called Xuanxiao, who is Qingyang; Qingyang was lowered to dwell by the Yangzi.
idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor lived on Xuanyuan Mound and married a daughter of the western mound people—Leizu, his chief consort—who bore two sons whose descendants would rule: the elder, Xuanxiao, also called Qingyang, was sent downriver to dwell along the Yangzi.

literal: The second was called Changyi and was lowered to dwell by the Ruo River.
idiomatic: The younger was Changyi, who was sent to dwell beside the Ruo River.

literal: Changyi took a woman of the Shu mountains named Changpu and begot Gaoyang; Gaoyang possessed sagely virtue.
idiomatic: Changyi married a Shu-shan woman called Changpu and fathered Gaoyang, who showed sage-like virtue.

literal: The Yellow Emperor died and was buried at Bridge Mountain.
idiomatic: When the Yellow Emperor died he was interred at Bridge Mountain.

literal: His grandson, the son of Changyi, Gaoyang, was established; this is Emperor Zhuanxu.
idiomatic: His grandson Gaoyang, Changyi’s son, succeeded him—this is Emperor Zhuanxu.

literal: Emperor Zhuanxu Gaoyang was the Yellow Emperor’s grandson and the son of Changyi.
idiomatic: Emperor Zhuanxu, whose dynastic name was Gaoyang, was grandson to the Yellow Emperor and son of Changyi.

literal: Still as an abyss, thereby possessing counsel; penetrating, thereby knowing affairs.
idiomatic: Deep as a pool, he formed plans; clear in judgment, he grasped every affair.

literal: He nurtured materials to match the land, carried seasons to image Heaven, relied on ghosts and spirits to regulate righteousness, ordered vapors to transform and teach, purified sincerity to sacrifice.
idiomatic: He developed the land’s resources, aligned the seasons with heaven’s pattern, invoked spirits to set right conduct, harmonized qi to teach the people, and offered sacrifice in unstained devotion.

literal: North he reached to Dark Mound, south to Jiaozhi, west to Drifting Sands, east to Coiling Tree.
idiomatic: His sway ran north to Dark Mound, south to Jiaozhi, west to the Drifting Sands, and east to Coiling Tree.

literal: Creatures that move and rest, gods great and small, wherever sun and moon shone, none failed to come under his grindstone.
idiomatic: Every moving and resting creature, every great or petty deity within the reach of sun and moon acknowledged his authority.

literal: Emperor Zhuanxu begot a son named Qiongchan.
idiomatic: Emperor Zhuanxu had a son called Qiongchan.

literal: Zhuanxu died, and the grandson of Xuanxiao, Gaoxin, was established; this is Emperor Ku.
idiomatic: When Zhuanxu died, Gaoxin, grandson of Xuanxiao, came to the throne—Emperor Ku.

literal: Emperor Ku Gaoxin was the Yellow Emperor’s great-grandson.
idiomatic: Emperor Ku, styled Gaoxin, was the Yellow Emperor’s great-grandson.

literal: Gaoxin’s father was called Jiaoji; Jiaoji’s father was Xuanxiao; Xuanxiao’s father was the Yellow Emperor.
idiomatic: Gaoxin’s father was Jiaoji; Jiaoji’s father was Xuanxiao; Xuanxiao’s father was the Yellow Emperor.

literal: From Xuanxiao and Jiaoji none attained the throne; only when Gaoxin mounted the imperial seat.
idiomatic: Neither Xuanxiao nor Jiaoji ever ruled; Gaoxin was the first of that line to take the throne.

literal: Gaoxin to Zhuanxu was a junior of the same clan.
idiomatic: Gaoxin was a younger clansman of Zhuanxu.

literal: Gaoxin was born numinous and pronounced his own name.
idiomatic: Gaoxin was born with an uncanny spirit and already knew his own name.

literal: Broadly he bestowed benefit on creatures and things, not on himself alone.
idiomatic: He spread blessings far and wide, never hoarding them for himself.

literal: Acute thereby to know what is distant, bright thereby to spy what is subtle.
idiomatic: Keen of hearing, he grasped distant events; clear of sight, he detected the smallest signs.

literal: He followed Heaven’s righteousness and knew the people’s urgencies.
idiomatic: He obeyed heaven’s right pattern and understood the people’s pressing needs.

literal: Humane yet awesome, gracious yet trustworthy; cultivating himself, all under Heaven submitted.
idiomatic: He combined benevolence with majesty, kindness with good faith; by perfecting his own person he won the submission of the realm.

literal: He took the land’s wealth and used it sparingly; he soothed and taught the myriad people and profitably instructed them; he paced sun and moon and saw them off and in; he clarified ghosts and spirits and respectfully served them.
idiomatic: He drew on earth’s bounty yet spent frugally; he nurtured and guided the myriad folk with kindly lessons; he tracked the courses of sun and moon; he clarified the cult of spirits and served them with reverence.

literal: His countenance was luxuriant; his virtue towered high.
idiomatic: His bearing was composed and stately; his virtue rose sheer like a peak.

literal: His movements matched the seasons; his garb matched the scholar-knight.
idiomatic: His every act matched the season; his dress befitted a true gentleman.

literal: Emperor Ku soaked the mean and reached all under Heaven; wherever sun and moon shone, wherever wind and rain arrived, none failed to follow and submit.
idiomatic: Emperor Ku held fast the golden mean until it flooded the world; wherever sun and moon shone or wind and rain reached, none refused his rule.

literal: Emperor Ku married a daughter of the Chenfeng clan and begot Fangxun.
idiomatic: Emperor Ku took a Chenfeng woman to wife and she bore Fangxun.

literal: He married a daughter of the Zouzi clan and begot Zhi.
idiomatic: He also married a Zouzi woman and she bore Zhi.

literal: Emperor Ku died and Zhi replaced him.
idiomatic: When Emperor Ku died, Zhi succeeded him.

literal: Emperor Zhi being established was not good; he died, and his younger brother Fangxun was established; this is Emperor Yao.
idiomatic: Zhi proved an unworthy ruler and soon died; his younger brother Fangxun took the throne—this is Emperor Yao.

literal: Emperor Yao was Fangxun.
idiomatic: Emperor Yao’s personal name was Fangxun.

literal: His benevolence was like Heaven; his knowledge was like a spirit’s.
idiomatic: His kindness embraced all like heaven itself; his wisdom pierced like a spirit’s.

literal: Approaching him was like the sun; gazing on him was like clouds.
idiomatic: To draw near him felt like standing in sunlight; to look on him was like beholding drifting clouds.

literal: Rich yet not proud, noble yet not slack.
idiomatic: Though wealthy he showed no arrogance; though exalted he never grew careless.

literal: Yellow hatbands and dark upper robes, red carriage, white horses.
idiomatic: He wore a dark robe with yellow turban bands and rode a vermilion chariot drawn by white horses.

literal: Able to brighten and tame virtue, thereby drawing close the nine kindreds.
idiomatic: He could refine his virtue and draw the nine agnates into harmony.

literal: The nine kindreds being harmonious, he then made brilliant the hundred surnames.
idiomatic: Once the nine branches of the clan were at peace, he brought light to every lineage.

literal: The hundred surnames being brilliant, he united and harmonized the myriad states.
idiomatic: When every house stood clear in duty, he knit the myriad states into concord.

literal: Thereupon he commanded Xi and He reverently to follow August Heaven, to number and model sun, moon, and stars, and reverently to hand down the seasons to the people.
idiomatic: He charged Xi and He to reverence High Heaven, to calculate the courses of sun, moon, and stars, and to teach the people the calendar in due season.

literal: Separately he commanded Xi Zhong to dwell at Yuanyi, called the Valley of Dawn.
idiomatic: He separately ordered Xi Zhong to reside at Yuanyi, the Bright Valley of the east.

literal: Reverently to mark the road of the rising sun and to arrange the labors of spring in the east.
idiomatic: There Xi Zhong was to reverence the sun’s rising path and set the spring work of the eastern quarter.

literal: When the sun stood at midcourse, the asterism Bird marked mid-spring.
idiomatic: At noon when the Bird asterism culminated, they fixed mid-spring.

literal: The people dispersed; birds and beasts bred and their coats grew fine.
idiomatic: The people went abroad to their tasks; birds and beasts paired and grew soft new fur.

literal: Separately he commanded Xi Shu to dwell at Southern Intersection.
idiomatic: He next ordered Xi Shu to take post at Southern Intersection.

literal: He arranged the labors of the south and reverently waited on the summer.
idiomatic: There Xi Shu arranged the southern tasks and reverently observed midsummer.

literal: The day longest, the asterism Fire marked mid-summer.
idiomatic: On the longest day, when the Fire asterism stood at zenith, they fixed midsummer.

literal: The people dwelt high; birds and beasts shed their coarse coats.
idiomatic: The people sought the heights; birds and beasts moulted their winter pelage.

literal: Separately he commanded He Zhong to dwell in the western lands, called the Valley of Gloom.
idiomatic: He then sent He Zhong west to the land called Gloom Valley.

literal: Reverently to mark the road of the setting sun and to arrange the harvest labors of the west.
idiomatic: He Zhong reverenced the sun’s setting path and ordered the autumn labors of the western quarter.

literal: Night at midpoint, the asterism Void marked mid-autumn.
idiomatic: At midnight when the Void asterism crossed the meridian, they fixed mid-autumn.

literal: The people were level and easy; birds and beasts had sleek new down.
idiomatic: The people grew calm; birds and beasts grew sleek new coats.

literal: Separately he commanded He Shu.
idiomatic: Finally he ordered He Shu.

literal: To dwell in the north, called Dark Capital.
idiomatic: He Shu was to dwell in the north at Dark Capital.

literal: To arrange the hiding things.
idiomatic: There he regulated the dormant creatures of winter.

literal: The day shortest, the asterism Mao marked mid-winter.
idiomatic: On the shortest day, when Mao culminated, they fixed midwinter.

literal: The people warmed themselves; birds and beasts grew thick down.
idiomatic: The people kept to the warmth indoors; birds and beasts grew heavy winter down.

literal: The year has three hundred sixty-six days; by intercalary months the four seasons are corrected.
idiomatic: The year was reckoned at three hundred sixty-six days, with intercalary months to true the four seasons.

literal: Faithfully he corrected the hundred offices, and every task flourished.
idiomatic: He disciplined the hundred bureaus with strict faithfulness, and every undertaking throve.

literal: Yao said, “Who may be entrusted with this affair?”
idiomatic: Yao asked, “Who can be charged with these affairs?”

literal: Fang Qi said, “The heir son Dan Zhu is enlightened.”
idiomatic: Fang Qi answered, “Your heir Dan Zhu is bright enough for the task.”

literal: Yao said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Yao sighed, “Ah—

literal: Stubborn and vicious—he cannot be used.”
idiomatic: the boy is stubborn and depraved; he will not do.”

literal: Yao again said, “Who may do it?”
idiomatic: Yao asked again, “Who then is fit?”

literal: Huan Dou said, “Gonggong everywhere gathers and displays merit; he may be used.”
idiomatic: Huan Dou proposed Gonggong: “He gathers merit on every side; appoint him.”

literal: Yao said, “Gonggong’s words are fair, but his deeds are crooked; he seems respectful yet he insults High Heaven—he cannot be used.”
idiomatic: Yao replied, “Gonggong talks fair but acts crooked; he feigns reverence while scorning heaven—he is unfit.”

literal: Yao again said, “Oh, you Four Peaks, the floodwaters surge to heaven; vast and wide they embrace hills and girdle heights; the lower people groan—who can be sent to govern them?”
idiomatic: Yao cried, “O Four Peaks, the deluge rolls to the very sky; it swallows hills and clasps ridges, and the people below lament—who can be sent to tame it?”

literal: All said Gun might.
idiomatic: All cried, “Let Gun try.”

literal: Yao said, “Gun defies commands and ruins his kin; he cannot be used.”
idiomatic: Yao said, “Gun defies orders and shames his clan; he will not do.”

literal: The Peaks said, “Different indeed—try him; if he fails, dismiss him.”
idiomatic: The Peaks urged, “At least try him—if he fails you may cast him aside.”

literal: Yao thereupon listened to the Peaks and employed Gun.
idiomatic: Yao yielded to the Peaks and gave Gun the work.

literal: Nine years, his task was not completed.
idiomatic: Nine years passed and Gun accomplished nothing.

literal: Yao said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Yao said, “Ah!

literal: You Four Peaks: I have sat on the throne seventy years—can you carry out my mandate and mount my place?”
idiomatic: O Four Peaks, seventy years I have held the throne—will any of you take my charge and mount my seat?”

literal: The Peaks answered, “Our virtue is mean; we shame the imperial seat.”
idiomatic: The Peaks answered, “Our virtue is too slight—we would disgrace the throne.”
